" * * *  
The adjacency matrix $A$ of the graph $G$ is defined such that $A_{ij} = w(e)$, where $w(e)$ is the weight of the edge from $i$ to $j$. The matrix exponential $e^A$ represents the expected number of times vertex $j$ can be reached from vertex $i$ in a continuous-time random walk, as it accounts for the time spent on each edge. <<The matrix exponential is a solution to the differential equation governing the random walk.>>  
To reduce storage, the specialist uses SVD on $A$ to decompose it into $U\Sigma V^T$, where $U$ and $V$ are orthogonal matrices and $\Sigma$ is a diagonal matrix of singular values. The optimal low-rank matrix $B_k$ is obtained by truncating $\Sigma$ to the first $k$ singular values. <<The Frobenius norm is minimized by retaining only the top $k$ singular values.>>  
The rank-reduced matrix $B_k$ approximates $A$ with fewer dimensions, reducing storage while preserving the essential structure of the original matrix. <<The Frobenius norm ensures that the approximation is as close as possible to the original matrix.>>"
